text: Go, Jeremias, and you who publicly adhere to me.<hr>Man. Sodom would have been spared for the sake of ten just people, Genesis 18:32. Before the reform of Josias, Jerusalem was strangely corrupted, though these expressions be hyperbolical, and spoken as it were in the heat of debate. Josias, Holda, and others, were living at this time, and renowned for their piety.
Falsely. Some may confess my name. But they swear falsely, Jeremias 4:2. (Calmet)<hr>Unless the requisite conditions be observed, an oath is unlawful. (Worthington)
Return. The miseries with which, Achaz, etc., were afflicted, produced no amendment.
Foolish idiots, (Calmet) who have had no means of receiving instruction. (Haydock)<hr>Such might be more excusable; but when the chiefs offend, the evil is irremediable.

Lion. Nabuchodonosor.<hr>Wolf. Cambyses, (Calmet) and leopard. Epiphanes. (Haydock)<hr>The Chaldeans, Persians and Greeks afflict them. (St. Jerome)<hr>The first beast may designate Nabuc: the second Nabuzardon: the third, Alexander [the Great?] or Epiphanes. (Worthington)
How can. I have only the character of judge left. (Calmet)
Stallions. Hebrew mashcim, (Haydock) "stretching out," as others translate, Ezechiel 23:20. (St. Jerome)<hr>The horse is the most intemperate of all animals but man. (Aristotle, Hist. 6:22.)

Destroy, at the first taking of the city, ver. 18., and Jeremias 4:27.<hr>Branches. Children of Jechonias, (chap. 22:30.) and the people. (Calmet)<hr>Hebrew, "her battlements," (Haydock) Chaldean, "palaces."
Them. The evil shall fall on the head of these impostors. So the wicked deride the prophets (Calmet) impugning the known truth. (Haydock)
Fire. Thou shalt declare their impending ruin.

Ancient. Nemrod founded the empire of Ninive and of Babylon, Genesis 10:10. Nabopolassar had succeeded to the ancient Assyrian and Chaldean sovereigns. Under his son, Nabuchodonosor, the dominions were much enlarged.<hr>Say. 4 Kings 18:26.
Devour. Hebrew, "destroy." They did not eat human flesh.
Own. You shall thus know the difference between the masters. (Calmet)
Understanding. Literally, "heart," Jeremias 4:22. (Haydock)
It. Nature and chance are improper terms. God regulates all, and has established those laws which preserve the world.

Rain, in autumn and in spring, Deuteronomy 11:14. (Calmet)<hr>Fulness. Hebrew, "the weeks for harvest." (Haydock)<hr>That of barley began at the Passover, and that of wheat ended before the feast of weeks, (Exodus 34:22., and Leviticus 23:10.; Calmet) or Pentecost. (Haydock)
Away. The rain comes unseasonably.
Man. As some tyrants have done, though this implies (Calmet) the eagerness with which the wicked strive to corrupt mankind. (Haydock)
Of the widow, is not in Hebrew. (Calmet)<hr>Fatherless. Protestants add, "yet they prosper, and they," etc. (Haydock)
Nation? It is contrary to God's justice not to punish the guilty. (Worthington)
Clapped. Hebrew, "came down to, or received in, or domineered by their hands." The priests concurred with these impostors for their own ends. (Calmet)
